stories.headline: Israel Intercepts Gaza Flotilla Near Crete, Detains 175 Activists in International Waters
stories.summary:
Israeli naval forces intercepted and boarded vessels from the Global Sumud Flotilla approximately 965 kilometers from Gaza in international waters west of Crete overnight, detaining roughly 175 activists from more than 20 boats. The flotilla, comprising 58 vessels that departed from Spain, France, and Italy two weeks ago, was attempting to break Israel's naval blockade of Gaza. Organizers accused Israeli forces of "piracy," alleging they smashed engines, jammed communications, and left some vessels stranded in the path of an approaching storm.

The Israeli Foreign Ministry defended the operation as lawful, saying it was conducted "peacefully and without any casualties" and that an initial inspection revealed what appeared to be drugs and contraband. Israel accused flotilla organizers of coordinating with Hamas to undermine President Trump's Gaza peace plan and distract from Hamas's refusal to disarm.

Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni condemned the seizure and demanded the immediate release of 24 detained Italian nationals, calling for "full respect of international law." The European Union echoed those demands, urging Israel to comply with international humanitarian and maritime law.

The incident threatens to escalate diplomatic tensions between Israel and several European Union member states. Greece has not publicly commented, though opposition politicians called on the government to explain its response to what they characterized as an illegal act.

stories.headline: India's Digital Fraud Crisis: $2.5 Billion Lost in 2025 as Central Bank Proposes Countermeasures
stories.summary:
India's Reserve Bank has released a discussion paper proposing new measures to combat a dramatic surge in digital payment fraud, after nearly 2.5 million Indians lost an estimated $2.5 billion in 2025 — a 4,300 percent increase since 2021. The explosive growth in fraud has accompanied India's rapid adoption of digital payments over the past five years, with scammers employing increasingly sophisticated "social engineering" tactics to drain bank accounts.

Proposed RBI measures include a one-hour delay on account-to-account transactions, additional authentication by a trusted third party for high-value payments by vulnerable populations such as the elderly, and new controls allowing users to switch digital payment capabilities on and off. The paper will be finalized after a public comment period.

Experts are cautiously skeptical. Rajesh Bansal, former CEO of the RBI's Innovation Hub, said OTP-based scams — the type the proposals primarily address — now represent a "minuscule proportion" of total fraud in value terms, as criminal tactics have evolved to more sophisticated methods. Implementation of transaction lags would also require significant changes to the existing payment architecture involving multiple parties.

The scale of the problem underscores broader risks accompanying the rapid digitization of financial services in large emerging economies, where consumer protections and fraud prevention infrastructure have struggled to keep pace with adoption rates.

stories.headline: Samsung's Succession Drama: Jailed Heir, Family Intrigue, and South Korea's Corporate Monarchy
stories.summary:
A detailed account of the Samsung conglomerate's complex dynastic succession struggle has drawn renewed attention to the case of Lee Jae-yong, the grandson of Samsung's founder who was jailed in 2017 for bribery in a scandal that also led to the impeachment of South Korean President Park Geun-hye. Lee, described by one analyst as "one of the most powerful people in the history of technology," had been consolidating family control over the conglomerate when prosecutors alleged he paid bribes in exchange for political support for a key merger.

The corruption case stemmed from a merger between Samsung C&T and Cheil Industries, which prosecutors argued was engineered through stock and accounting fraud to maximize Lee's control over Samsung Electronics. Lee has consistently denied the fraud charges, though he was found guilty of bribery. The 2016 scandal sparked weeks of mass protests in Seoul involving millions of citizens.

Samsung's governance structure reflects a broader pattern in South Korean chaebol — large family-controlled conglomerates — where intricate webs of cross-shareholdings and mergers are used to maintain dynastic control. The company comprises dozens of subsidiaries spanning electronics, construction, insurance, and retail, making succession both financially and legally complicated.

The case highlights ongoing questions about corporate governance, accountability, and the outsized influence of family-controlled conglomerates in South Korea's economy, where Samsung alone accounts for a significant share of the country's GDP and exports.

stories.headline: Scientists Race to Engineer Perfect Turf for 2026 FIFA World Cup Across 16 North American Venues
stories.summary:
With the FIFA Men's World Cup set to kick off across 16 venues in Canada, Mexico, and the United States this summer, sports turf scientists are working to ensure consistent playing surfaces across wildly different climates and stadium types. University of Tennessee turf specialist John Sorochan is leading the effort, which must reconcile natural grass requirements in open-air tropical stadiums with indoor domed venues and high-altitude sites.

The challenge is compounded by the fact that eight of the 16 host stadiums use artificial turf and must be temporarily converted to natural grass for the tournament. Five of those are indoor domes, requiring specially engineered grass installations. Sorochan's team has developed specialized testing equipment — including a "flex machine" with a 3D-printed foot — to measure how playing surfaces interact with athletes' bodies and standardize bounce characteristics across all venues.

FIFA requires balls dropped from two meters to bounce back between 0.6 and one meter, and surface compliance must be uniform enough to prevent injury differences between venues. The grass varieties differ significantly: warm-season Bermuda grass suited to Miami's sea-level heat versus cool-season varieties appropriate for Vancouver's climate.

The World Cup final is expected to draw roughly 20 percent of the global population as viewers, placing enormous pressure on every logistical detail, including the playing surface itself.

stories.headline: Update: Islamist Militants Tighten Total Blockade on Mali's Capital Bamako
stories.summary:
Islamist fighters from Jama'at Nusrat al-Islam wal-Muslimin (JNIM) have imposed a near-total blockade on Bamako, Mali's capital of more than three million people, closing at least three of the city's six main entry routes for extended periods. The blockade follows a Wednesday warning by JNIM that 'no-one will be allowed in any more,' leaving residents stranded on highways and cutting off food and fuel supplies. A total blockade has also been imposed on Ségou, a town roughly 50 miles from the capital, where hundreds of vehicles and passengers have been stuck for days without adequate access to food or water.

The crisis escalated after last weekend's coordinated nationwide attacks by an alliance of jihadists and the separatist Azawad Liberation Front (FLA), which seeks to overthrow the military regime of Gen. Assimi Goïta. The FLA has seized the northern city of Kidal, forcing Russian Africa Corps fighters — successors to the Wagner Group who have backed the junta — to withdraw from the area. The FLA has since demanded a full withdrawal of Russian forces from Mali and vowed to march on additional northern cities.

Junta leader Goïta convened an emergency security council meeting Wednesday and claimed armed forces had inflicted 'heavy losses' on rebels, while vowing tough action. A Kremlin spokesperson countered that Russian forces would remain in Mali 'to combat extremism, terrorism and other harmful phenomena.' Analysts warn the blockade signals significant regime weakness, with one French foreign affairs think-tank director telling the BBC the moves show 'the regime is weak and can't restore security.'

This story builds on earlier coverage of last weekend's coordinated rebel and jihadist attacks that seized key cities. The new development is the imposition of a near-total blockade on Bamako itself, marking a significant escalation that directly threatens the capital's civilian population and exposes the limits of both the Malian military and its Russian backers.

stories.headline: Body Found in Australian Outback Believed to Be Abducted Five-Year-Old Girl; Manhunt Continues
stories.summary:
Australian police say they have found a body they believe to be that of Kumanjayi Little Baby, a five-year-old Aboriginal girl who was allegedly abducted from her home near Alice Springs on Saturday. Northern Territory Police announced Thursday that forensic testing is underway to confirm the child's identity and cause of death. Investigation lead Peter Malley told reporters bluntly: 'We believed he has murdered this child.'

Authorities have been searching for Jefferson Lewis, 47, who was released from prison just six days before the girl disappeared and was seen holding hands with her late on the night she vanished. Lewis has a history of domestic and family violence-related offences. Police say a pair of children's underwear found near the camp contained DNA from both the girl and Lewis, strengthening the case against him.

The manhunt has been complicated by Lewis's lack of a digital footprint — he has no phone, no bank account, and no car — forcing investigators to revert to labor-intensive methods. 'It's going back to 1930s policing,' Malley said Wednesday. Police believe Lewis has been assisted by others and have appealed for information, warning they are 'knocking on doors' and 'going through houses.'

Around 200 police and volunteers have searched the area for five days. Chief Minister Lia Finocchiaro called it 'the worst possible outcome,' and the girl's mother released a statement saying, 'It is going to be so hard to live the rest of our lives without you.' The case has renewed scrutiny of recidivism and community safety policies in Australia's Northern Territory.

stories.headline: ECB's Lagarde Joins Powell in Rejecting 1970s-Style Stagflation Comparison for Current Economy
stories.summary:
European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde said Thursday that the term 'stagflation' belongs to the 1970s and should not be applied to the current economic situation in the euro zone. Her remarks echo similar pushback from U.S. Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, who has also resisted characterizing today's conditions as analogous to the prolonged period of simultaneous high inflation and economic stagnation that defined that decade.

The comments come amid growing concern among economists and market participants that U.S. tariff policy — and the uncertainty surrounding global trade — could push major economies toward slower growth alongside persistent inflation, conditions that superficially resemble stagflation. Both central bank chiefs appear intent on managing expectations and avoiding language that could heighten market anxiety or complicate their monetary policy mandates.

Lagarde's statement carries particular weight as the ECB navigates slowing growth in Germany and other major euro zone economies while keeping a close eye on inflation, which has moderated significantly from its post-pandemic peak but remains a policy concern. The alignment between the two most powerful central bankers in the world on this framing is notable and may be intended to project confidence in the resilience of current economic structures compared to those of the 1970s.

stories.headline: Canada Eyes Arctic Port Expansion in Churchill as Trade Diversification Push Intensifies
stories.summary:
Canada is moving forward with ambitious plans to expand the Port of Churchill in northern Manitoba, the country's only Arctic deep-water seaport, as Prime Minister Mark Carney pushes to double non-U.S. exports within a decade. The federal government has already invested C$320 million in the port and views it as a potential gateway for shipping critical minerals, grain, liquefied natural gas, and other commodities more quickly to Europe, Africa, and South America via Hudson Bay and the North Atlantic.

The expansion effort is driven by a confluence of factors: the economic disruption of U.S. tariffs, Europe's energy needs amid ongoing global conflicts, and the gradual opening of Arctic shipping lanes due to climate change. Churchill, known internationally as the 'Polar Bear Capital of the World,' has a population of roughly 1,000 and has long depended on seasonal wildlife tourism, but local leaders see the port as a transformational economic opportunity capable of creating hundreds of jobs.

The port has had a troubled history, falling into disrepair under a Denver-based private owner before a consortium of indigenous and community groups — the Arctic Gateway Group — took ownership in 2018. The port had previously been used mainly to export prairie grain but shut down in 2016 as shipments declined, reopening in 2019. Logistical challenges remain significant, as the port is only operational for four to five months per year due to Arctic conditions.

Experts have historically questioned whether a Churchill expansion makes sound economic sense, given the seasonal limitations and infrastructure demands. But advocates argue that geopolitical shifts and Canada's desire to reduce trade dependence on the United States have fundamentally changed the calculus, making the project more viable and politically urgent than at any point in its history.

stories.headline: First Trump Tariff Refunds Expected Around May 11, Court Documents Show
stories.summary:
The federal government is expected to begin issuing the first refunds of President Trump's court-invalidated tariffs on or about May 11, according to newly released court documents. U.S. Court of International Trade Judge Richard Eaton disclosed the timeline following a closed hearing Wednesday.

Customs and Border Protection has been working for weeks to process approximately $166 billion in tariff refunds stemming from the Supreme Court's ruling striking down the levies. The timeline provides the first concrete date for businesses that have been awaiting reimbursement amid ongoing uncertainty over U.S. trade policy.

The pending refunds add a new layer of complexity to an already unsettled economic environment, as companies simultaneously face the prospect of new tariffs while awaiting reimbursement for those already paid. Businesses such as toy manufacturer Basic Fun! have been working to document and file claims ahead of the refund process.

stories.headline: Becerra, Steyer Battle for Momentum at California Governor's Debate
stories.summary:
Top Democratic candidates for California governor clashed in a debate this week with less than a week before voters begin receiving mail ballots, as the crowded field adjusts to the recent exit of former Rep. Eric Swalwell. Former California Attorney General Xavier Becerra and billionaire climate activist Tom Steyer emerged as the leading competitors vying for Democratic consolidation.

Becerra, who surged in polling following Swalwell's withdrawal, is leaning on his record as a former U.S. attorney general and longtime California official. Steyer, a major Democratic donor and climate investor, is positioning himself as an outsider willing to challenge entrenched interests, describing himself as a 'class traitor' on economic issues.

The California governor's race carries national significance as the state's large Democratic electorate and its role as a progressive policy laboratory make it a bellwether for the party's direction. The primary outcome could shape California's approach to issues ranging from housing and homelessness to energy policy and relations with Washington.

stories.headline: China Bans Drone Sales in Beijing, Requires Permits to Fly
stories.summary:
China's capital Beijing is implementing sweeping new drone regulations taking effect Friday, prohibiting the sale, rental, or import of drones and requiring permits for all outdoor flights. Drone owners must register devices with police and complete an online training course and regulatory test before operating them.

The rules represent a significant tightening of an already restrictive framework. Last year Beijing designated its entire airspace a no-drone zone requiring air traffic authority approval for flights. DJI, the world's largest drone manufacturer and a Beijing retailer, is reportedly pulling products from shelves ahead of the new rules.

China has more than three million registered drones and has identified a 'low-altitude economy' — encompassing drones and flying taxis — as a strategic priority projected to generate over two trillion yuan ($290 billion) by 2035. Authorities say the restrictions aim to balance public safety with continued technological and economic development, with exemptions carved out for counter-terrorism and disaster relief applications.

stories.headline: Switzerland to Vote on Proposal Capping National Population at 10 Million
stories.summary:
Swiss voters are set to weigh a referendum proposal that would constitutionally cap the country's population at 10 million people — a measure that would require dramatic reductions in immigration and could upend Switzerland's relationship with the European Union and international labor markets. The country's current population stands at approximately 8.9 million.

The vote reflects broader pressure across Europe from rising far-right political parties pushing governments toward stricter immigration controls. Switzerland, which is not an EU member but has bilateral agreements allowing free movement with the bloc, would face significant treaty complications if the cap were adopted and enforced.

Proponents argue the cap is necessary to manage housing shortages, infrastructure strain, and cultural cohesion. Critics warn the measure is unworkable, legally incompatible with existing international commitments, and could severely damage the Swiss economy, which relies heavily on foreign skilled workers in sectors including finance, pharmaceuticals, and technology.

stories.headline: U.S. Sanctions Major Chinese Refinery and 40 Shippers Over Iranian Oil Trade
stories.summary:
The Trump administration announced sweeping secondary sanctions Wednesday targeting a major China-based oil refinery and approximately 40 shipping companies and tankers involved in transporting Iranian crude oil. The centerpiece of the action is Hengli Petrochemical's facility in Dalian, one of China's largest independent refineries with a processing capacity of roughly 400,000 barrels per day, which Treasury says has received Iranian oil since 2023 and generated hundreds of millions of dollars in revenue for the Iranian military.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the department 'will continue to constrict the network of vessels, intermediaries and buyers Iran relies on to move its oil to global markets.' The move follows letters sent earlier this month to financial institutions in China, Hong Kong, the UAE and Oman warning of secondary sanctions for facilitating Iranian transactions. China is the destination for an estimated 80–90 percent of Iranian oil exports, often with origin obscured through a shadow fleet.

The sanctions arrive weeks before a scheduled meeting between President Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping, adding economic pressure to an already strained bilateral relationship. They also coincide with a U.S. physical blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, which has sent global oil prices sharply higher. Iran has previously said lifting sanctions is a precondition for ending the war with the U.S. and Israel.

The action marks an escalation in the administration's 'maximum pressure' campaign against Tehran and directly implicates Beijing's state-adjacent energy sector. Analysts note the timing — just before the Trump-Xi summit — suggests Washington is willing to use the Iran conflict as leverage in broader trade and geopolitical negotiations with China.

stories.headline: NHTSA Bans Chinese Airbag Inflators Linked to at Least 10 Deaths
stories.summary:
The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration on Wednesday banned Chinese-made airbag inflators tied to at least 10 fatalities and two serious injuries in the United States. NHTSA's investigation found that the frontal driver airbags exploded upon deployment, sending large metal fragments into drivers' chests, necks, eyes and faces. All documented fatal incidents involved General Motors or Hyundai vehicles.

The ban prohibits the import and sale of the affected Chinese-manufactured inflators and is part of a broader federal scrutiny of Chinese automotive components. The action comes amid heightened concerns over supply chain safety and national security in the auto sector. A bipartisan Senate bill to ban the importation of Chinese-made vehicles and auto parts was introduced separately in recent weeks.

The defect draws parallels to the Takata airbag scandal, which led to the largest automotive recall in U.S. history and multiple deaths from rupturing inflators. Safety advocates say the NHTSA action underscores the risks of relying on foreign-manufactured safety-critical components. The agency did not immediately specify how many vehicles on U.S. roads contain the banned inflators or whether a recall would follow.

stories.headline: Press Freedom Falls to 25-Year Low, With U.S. Dropping Seven Places, Watchdog Says
stories.summary:
Press freedom worldwide has reached its lowest point in a quarter century, Reporters Without Borders (RSF) warned Thursday in its annual index. For the first time in the index's 25-year history, more than half of the 180 countries surveyed now fall into the 'difficult' or 'very serious' categories, and the share of the global population living in a country with 'good' press freedom has plummeted from 20 percent to less than 1 percent, with only seven Northern European nations — led by Norway — qualifying.

The United States dropped seven additional places to 64th, having already fallen from a 'fairly good' to a 'problematic' rating in 2024. RSF cited what it described as President Trump's 'systematic' attacks on journalists, the detention and expulsion of Salvadoran journalist Mario Guevara, and drastic cuts to funding for U.S. international broadcasting as key factors driving the decline. The White House has not formally responded to the rankings.

The steepest single-country decline was recorded in junta-led Niger, which fell 37 places to 120th, reflecting a broader deterioration across the Sahel region. Russia ranked 172nd, with RSF noting Moscow holds 48 journalists behind bars and has weaponized anti-terrorism and extremism laws to suppress independent reporting. Saudi Arabia was also cited after executing a journalist in 2025.

Among the few bright spots, post-Assad Syria recorded the index's largest improvement, climbing 36 places as restrictions eased following the fall of the previous government. China, North Korea and Eritrea — ranked last for the third consecutive year — showed virtually no change, reflecting entrenched state control over information.

stories.headline: Outlaw Country Pioneer David Allan Coe Dies at 86
stories.summary:
David Allan Coe, a foundational figure in outlaw country music known as the 'outlaw's outlaw,' died Wednesday at age 86 in intensive care, according to his representative. His widow, Kimberly, confirmed the death to Rolling Stone, calling him 'one of the best singers, songwriters, and performers of our time.' No cause of death was immediately released.

Coe was born in Akron, Ohio, in 1939 and spent part of his youth in correctional facilities before moving to Nashville to pursue music. He released his debut album, 'Penitentiary Blues,' in 1970 and went on to write Tanya Tucker's No. 1 hit 'Would You Lay With Me (In a Field of Stone)' in 1973 and scored a top-10 hit of his own with 'You Never Even Called Me By My Name' in 1975. Later career highlights included 'The Ride' and 'Mona Lisa Lost Her Smile' in the 1980s, as well as an unlikely collaboration with members of heavy metal band Pantera.

Coe was known for controversial material involving drug use and explicit content, and faced legal troubles in the 2010s, pleading guilty in 2015 to tax-related charges and being ordered to pay nearly $1 million in back taxes. He had also experienced significant health issues in recent years, including a bout with COVID-19 in 2021. He is survived by his widow.

stories.headline: LAPD Chief Warns Los Angeles Not Prepared to Secure 2028 Olympics Due to Staffing Shortages
stories.summary:
Los Angeles Police Chief Jim McDonnell told the City Council Budget and Finance Committee on Wednesday that the LAPD lacks the resources and dedicated funding necessary to properly secure the 2028 Olympic Games. McDonnell said organizer LA28 has "zero police or other safety budgets," with existing security funding shared across multiple agencies and restricted primarily to officer overtime rather than covering full law enforcement needs.

The department's Olympic Special Events Unit has determined roughly 6,700 officers will be needed across eight venues during the Games, along with 700 to 800 additional patrol vehicles — costs that are not fully funded. The LAPD is already losing more than 500 officers per year to attrition and expects to log approximately 1.4 million overtime hours this year, contributing to a projected $16.5 million deficit.

Olympic organizers have maintained that federal involvement, given the Games' designation as a National Special Security Event, will address security gaps. LAPD officials pushed back on that claim, calling it "inaccurate." City Council member Eunisses Hernandez questioned whether the full scale of police deployment was necessary, asking whether school buses could substitute for police vehicles.

The warnings come amid heightened national concern over security at large public events following recent incidents. The 2028 Los Angeles Olympics are expected to draw athletes and visitors from around the world, making security planning a matter of both local budget politics and national preparedness.

stories.headline: Singer D4vd Allegedly Bought Chainsaws, Body Bag Online to Dispose of Stabbed Teen Girl, Prosecutors Allege
stories.summary:
New court filings released Wednesday allege that rising pop singer D4vd — legal name David Anthony Burke — stabbed 14-year-old Celeste Rivas Hernandez multiple times at his Hollywood Hills home, then purchased tools including two chainsaws, an inflatable pool, a body bag, a shovel, and a "burn cage" online under a fake name in an effort to dispose of her remains. The dismembered body of Rivas Hernandez was found in Burke's Tesla in September 2025.

Prosecutors allege Burke met Rivas Hernandez when she was 11 years old and began sexually abusing her at age 13. They say the teen sent text messages on April 22, 2025, threatening to expose their relationship and "destroy his life," and that she was killed the following day after Burke summoned her to his home via an Uber. The evidence filing provides the most detailed public account yet of the alleged crime and alleged cover-up.

Burke appeared in court Wednesday in an orange jail jumpsuit and shackles, agreeing to a delay of his preliminary hearing until late May. He has pleaded not guilty, and his attorneys stated they "will vigorously defend David's innocence" and believe "the evidence will show that David did not kill Celeste."

D4vd had gained widespread recognition as an independent artist before his arrest. His first studio album was released on April 25, 2025 — the same week prosecutors allege the killing took place. The case has drawn significant public attention given his profile in the music industry.

stories.headline: Father and Daughter Plead Guilty to Selling Forged Warhols, Banksys and Picassos to NYC Art World
stories.summary:
A New Jersey father and daughter have pleaded guilty in federal court to running a years-long counterfeiting scheme that defrauded prominent New York art galleries and auction houses by selling forged paintings attributed to high-profile artists including Andy Warhol, Banksy, and Pablo Picasso. Erwin Bankowski, 50, and Karolina Bankowska, 26, admitted to commissioning an artist in Poland to produce the forgeries.

Federal prosecutors say the pair deceived some of New York's most prominent fine art auction houses as part of the scheme, which spanned multiple years. The Bankowskis apologized in court following their guilty pleas. Specific financial figures related to the fraud were not immediately disclosed in available reporting.

The case highlights ongoing vulnerabilities in the authentication processes used by even well-established corners of the art market. Forgery schemes involving works attributed to modern and contemporary artists — whose originals can sell for millions — have historically been difficult to detect, particularly when paired with convincing documentation.

The guilty pleas mark a significant resolution to what prosecutors described as a calculated fraud on a major cultural and commercial sector. Sentencing dates were not immediately reported.

stories.headline: France Investigates Reappearance of Website Linked to Pelicot Mass Rape Case
stories.summary:
French prosecutors have opened an investigation into the reappearance of an online platform closely resembling Coco.gg, the unmoderated chatroom website linked to the crimes of convicted mass rapist Dominique Pelicot and cited in more than 23,000 criminal reports before its 2024 shutdown. At least two similarly named sites — Cocoland.cc and a related domain — appeared online in early April with near-identical designs to the original platform.

The Paris prosecutor's office confirmed an investigation has been opened for "disseminating violent, pornographic, or offensive messages accessible to minors." Journalists from French outlet BFM reported that posing as a 13-year-old girl, they were immediately contacted by users sending lewd photos and explicit messages even after disclosing the fake minor's age, with no registration required to access the site.

Coco.gg became internationally notorious during the 2024 trial of Dominique Pelicot, who was convicted of drugging and raping his wife Gisèle over a decade and recruiting more than 50 strangers via the platform's chatrooms. Forty-nine men were also convicted. The founder of the original platform, Isaac Steidl, was charged in January 2025 with multiple offenses including child pornography; his lawyer says he has "nothing to do" with the new sites.

France's high commissioner for childhood, Sarah El Haïry, called the reappearance a "collective failure" and said she had filed complaints against two additional open-chatroom websites. She said children were being "approached by predators" and placed responsibility on both website creators and the platforms hosting them.

stories.headline: Kenya's Sabastian Sawe Returns Home to Jubilant Celebrations After Sub-Two-Hour Marathon
stories.summary:
Kenyan marathon runner Sabastian Sawe returned to Nairobi on Thursday to an outpouring of national celebration following his historic sub-two-hour finish at the London Marathon on Sunday — the first time any man has broken the two-hour barrier in official competitive race conditions. Sawe crossed the finish line in 1:59:30, more than a minute faster than the previous world record of 2:00:35 set by the late Kelvin Kiptum in 2023.

Sawe's Kenya Airways flight received a water cannon salute upon landing at Jomo Kenyatta International Airport, where dancers, musicians, cameras, and family members — including his parents, who drove six hours for the occasion — greeted him. "I am happy about this good day, that you came to celebrate with me, I did not expect it," Sawe told the crowd.

The 31-year-old, who has now won all four marathons he has entered, ran the second half of the London race in 59:01 — faster than his first half split of 1:00:29. He is expected to meet Kenyan President William Ruto and has already spoken about competing again in the autumn, saying he believes he can run an even faster time.

Sawe's achievement is considered the holy grail of marathon running, a barrier that eluded the sport for generations. Unlike Eliud Kipchoge's sub-two-hour run in 2019, which was an unofficial time trial, Sawe's time was set under standard competitive race conditions and counts as an official world record.

stories.headline: France Investigates Trump's Proposed Washington Monument Modeled on Arc de Triomphe
stories.summary:
President Donald Trump's vow to construct a triumphal arch in Washington that surpasses the Arc de Triomphe in Paris has drawn widespread skepticism and eye rolls in France, according to reporting published Thursday. Trump made the declaration to European leaders in January, and French reaction has ranged from bemused to dismissive.

The Arc de Triomphe, commissioned by Napoleon Bonaparte in 1806 and completed in 1836, stands 50 meters tall and serves as one of the most recognizable monuments in the world, honoring those who fought and died for France. The structure sits at the center of twelve radiating avenues at the Place de l'Étoile, and its construction carried deep political and nationalistic symbolism tied to French military history.

Trump's proposal has not yet advanced to formal planning stages, and no site, funding mechanism, or timeline has been publicly announced. The idea is being tracked alongside other large-scale infrastructure and monument proposals the administration has floated since taking office.

French commentators have noted the complicated and often contested history of such monuments — built variously to project imperial power, commemorate the dead, or serve propaganda purposes — suggesting any American counterpart would invite similar interpretive debates.

stories.headline: Update: U.S. War With Iran Has Cost $25 Billion in Nine Weeks, Pentagon Official Tells Congress
stories.summary:
A senior Pentagon official provided Congress with the first official cost estimate for the ongoing U.S. military conflict with Iran, putting the price tag at $25 billion — roughly equivalent to NASA's entire annual budget. The disclosure came during budget hearings on Capitol Hill as the war approaches its 60-day mark, now in its ninth week.

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth made his first public appearance on Capitol Hill since the war began, and clashed with lawmakers from both parties who have raised questions about the conflict's scope, cost, and impact on global munitions stockpiles. Congressional concern has grown over the depletion of U.S. and allied weapons inventories and the capacity to replenish them.

The $25 billion figure is expected to fuel intensifying debate in Congress over the war's authorization, duration, and strategic objectives. Republican and Democratic lawmakers alike have pressed the administration for a clearer exit strategy, while the administration has defended the military campaign as necessary to counter Iranian aggression and its nuclear program.

The cost revelation comes as the conflict continues with no ceasefire in sight, and as the administration faces pressure on multiple fronts — including from allies concerned about regional escalation and the strain on Western defense supply chains.

stories.headline: U.S. Charges Sitting Mexican Governor, Nine Other Officials With Aiding Sinaloa Cartel
stories.summary:
Federal prosecutors in New York have indicted Rubén Rocha Moya, the sitting governor of the Mexican state of Sinaloa, along with nine other current and former Mexican officials, on charges of conspiring with the Sinaloa Cartel to import narcotics into the United States. The indictment alleges the officials received political support and bribes in exchange for protecting cartel operations, specifically shielding the Los Chapitos faction of the Sinaloa Cartel.

The charges also name a sitting senator, a mayor, and a high-ranking police official. DEA Administrator Terrance Cole said the Sinaloa Cartel — designated a foreign terrorist organization — "relies on corruption and bribery to drive violence and profit." U.S. Attorney for the Southern District of New York Jay Clayton said corrupt politicians are essential to the cartel's operations.

The Mexican government pushed back sharply, with the Foreign Ministry stating that documents received from the U.S. embassy "do not include the elements of proof" required to support arrest and extradition requests. Governor Rocha Moya denied the charges "categorically and absolutely," framing the indictment as a political attack on Mexico's ruling party and its "Fourth Transformation" agenda. Mexican authorities separately announced their own investigation to assess whether the U.S. accusations have legal merit.

The indictment of a sitting governor from President Claudia Sheinbaum's party represents one of the most significant legal confrontations in the bilateral relationship in years, escalating tensions between Washington and Mexico City at a time when the two governments are already at odds over trade, immigration, and cartel policy.

stories.headline: Update: Prosecutors Release New Evidence Showing WHCD Shooting Suspect Armed in Hotel Room Before Attack
stories.summary:
Federal prosecutors have filed new evidence in the case against Cole Tomas Allen, the 31-year-old accused of opening fire at the White House Correspondents' Dinner on April 25, including photographs showing him posing in his hotel room with multiple weapons strapped to his body approximately 90 minutes before the attack. The images show Allen in dress clothes wearing a shoulder holster, carrying a sheathed knife, and holding a bag with ammunition.

Prosecutors allege Allen spent the half-hour before the attack browsing websites for live coverage of the dinner and checking on President Trump's attendance. He then allegedly discarded a long black coat concealing a pump-action shotgun before rushing a security checkpoint with the weapon raised. A Secret Service agent was shot but not seriously wounded; Allen faces life in prison if convicted on the attempted assassination charge, to which he has pleaded not guilty.

The government's memorandum, filed to support Allen's continued pretrial detention, also reveals that he kept notes on his phone documenting observations during his cross-country drive from California to Washington. Prosecutors argue the planning, travel, and preparation demonstrate the attack was "premeditated, violent, and calculated to cause death."

Allen faces multiple charges including attempted assassination of the president, interstate transportation of a firearm to commit a felony, and discharging a firearm during a crime of violence. His attorneys have not yet publicly responded to the new filing.

stories.headline: Australia's Bondi Beach Massacre Inquiry Finds Jewish Agency Warned Police of 'High' Threat Level Before Attack
stories.summary:
An interim report from Australia's royal commission into the Bondi Beach massacre has found that a Jewish security agency warned New South Wales Police of a "high" threat level — stating that "a terrorist attack against the NSW Jewish Community is likely" — just six days before two gunmen killed 15 people at a Hanukkah event on December 14, 2025. The commission, led by former High Court judge Virginia Bell, was announced in January following the attack.

Despite the warning email from the Community Security Group NSW on December 8, NSW Police told the commission it received "no specific request for dedicated police resources" for the Hanukkah event. The commission noted the discrepancy between accounts from police and the security group could not be resolved with available information. Sajid Akram was killed by police at the scene; his son Naveed Akram, 24, has been charged with 59 offences including 15 counts of murder and one count of committing a terrorist attack.

The report made 14 recommendations, including prioritizing gun reforms, a gun buyback scheme, extending policing arrangements from Jewish high holy days to other high-risk Jewish events, and making the counter-terrorism commissioner's role full-time. Five recommendations remain confidential for national security reasons. Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said a National Security Committee meeting had agreed to implement all of Bell's recommendations.

The findings have renewed scrutiny of how intelligence warnings are communicated and acted upon ahead of events at risk of attack. The report noted that gun reform responses from some states and territories have been "lukewarm," with Queensland refusing both the buyback scheme and a proposed cap on firearms ownership.

stories.headline: Update: Hungary's Prime Minister-Elect Magyar Meets EU Leaders, Says Frozen Funds 'Will Soon Arrive'
stories.summary:
Hungary's Prime Minister-elect Péter Magyar traveled to Brussels on Wednesday for his first meetings with European Union leadership since his Tisza party won a landslide election on April 12, ending 16 years of rule by Viktor Orbán. After talks with European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen, Magyar declared the discussions "extremely constructive and successful" and said that "EU resources will soon arrive in Hungary."

Magyar has pledged to unlock billions of euros in EU funds frozen during the Orbán era over concerns about corruption and democratic backsliding. His most pressing deadline is unlocking €10.4 billion from the EU's Covid-19 recovery fund before it expires at the end of August, contingent on meeting anti-corruption and rule-of-law milestones. An additional €6.3 billion in cohesion funds and €16.1 billion in cheap EU defense loans could also become available, along with an end to a €1 million daily fine Hungary has been paying for breaching EU migration rules.

Von der Leyen said the Commission would "support your work to address these issues and realign with shared European values." Magyar assured Hungarians that the EU was not imposing conditions contrary to their national interests, framing the reforms as straightforward: no corruption and no government interference with the courts. He is expected to return to Brussels on May 25 to sign a formal political agreement and is not scheduled to be sworn in until May 9.

Magyar's Tisza party secured a two-thirds "super-majority" in parliament, giving it the constitutional authority to implement the sweeping reforms the EU requires. Hungary's economy has shown minimal growth for three years, and Magyar has said the incoming funds are essential to its revival.

stories.headline: Christchurch Mosque Killer Brenton Tarrant Loses Appeal, Court Rules Claims 'Utterly Devoid of Merit'
stories.summary:
New Zealand's Court of Appeal has unanimously rejected white supremacist Brenton Tarrant's bid to overturn his conviction and sentence for the 2019 Christchurch mosque massacres, in which he killed 51 people and attempted to kill 40 others at Al Noor mosque and Linwood Islamic Centre. Tarrant, 35, is serving a life sentence with no possibility of parole — the first such sentence ever imposed in New Zealand.

In a week-long hearing held in February, Tarrant argued he was incapable of rational decision-making when he entered his guilty pleas because of "torturous and inhumane" prison conditions. A panel of three judges rejected that argument, finding his claims inconsistent and not supported by witness accounts, and ruling that he was not "coerced or pressured in any way" to plead guilty. The court said his arguments were "utterly devoid of merit" and that the facts of his crimes were "beyond dispute."

Family members of victims expressed relief at the ruling. Aya al-Umari, whose brother Hussein was killed in the attack, said she was "pleased and relieved" and that the decision confirmed justice was being served. She noted that proceedings like the appeal hearing force survivors to relive the trauma, but said the outcome offered "reassurance and comfort around the right processes being followed."

The March 2019 attack, parts of which Tarrant livestreamed, prompted New Zealand's parliament to vote within a month to ban military-style semi-automatic weapons and launch a firearms buyback scheme. Tarrant, an Australian national who moved to New Zealand in 2017, posted a racist manifesto online shortly before the attack.
